CORRECTED (Stage 3): 2.8% comparison acknowledged as rhetorically loaded — cumulative stock vs single-year flow. Now presented with caveat.

ADDED (Stage 4): CIPS participation — Malaysian affiliates of Chinese banks are direct CIPS members.

cards.text: Malaysia has settled RM82.1 billion in trade using local currencies with China, Thailand, and Indonesia. The growth is real — 22.8x since 2009. But 76% is with one country, and the infrastructure to manage that exposure barely exists.
cards.sub: PM Anwar disclosed the cumulative figure in a March 2026 parliamentary reply. China accounts for RM62.6 billion, with local currency settlement reaching 25.6% of bilateral trade — up from 1.2% in 2009. Thailand is at 17.2%, Indonesia at 15.8%. Against Malaysia's RM2.879 trillion in annual trade (2024), the 16-year cumulative total is roughly one month's trade value. The direction is clear. The scale is early-stage. And 74.4% of Malaysia-China trade is still settled in USD.

cards.h: The cumulative RM82.1B over 16 years equals roughly one month of Malaysia's annual trade — but within the China corridor, local currency is now a quarter of all transactions
cards.s: The cumulative-vs-annual comparison is imperfect — one is a stock, the other a flow — but it illustrates how early this transition is. The more meaningful metric: 25.6% of Malaysia-China bilateral trade is now settled in local currencies, up from 1.2% in 2009. Cross-border RMB transactions hit a record 102 billion yuan in Q1 2025, up 27% year-on-year. SMEs using the framework save up to 2% in transaction fees by avoiding double conversion (ringgit → USD → yuan becomes ringgit → yuan directly). But palm oil, LNG, and petroleum remain benchmarked in USD. Even when settled in ringgit, the price reference is dollar-denominated — the exchange rate risk shifts rather than disappears. For B40 households, the practical impact is not which currency settles trade but whether the ringgit buys less each month. The ringgit hit a 26-year low of 4.7965/USD in February 2024. Imported inflation — food, fuel, consumer goods — is the cost ordinary Malaysians pay for currency weakness that de-dollarization rhetoric does not address.
cards.d: Sources: Parliamentary reply (Mar 24, 2026), MATRADE (2024 trade), FMT (Feb 2026 — Q1 2025 RMB data), FMM (SME savings). CORRECTED: removed 'rounding error' framing per Stage 2; added B40 impact per Stage 2/5; acknowledged cumulative-vs-annual comparison caveat per Stage 3.

cards.h: 76% concentration in China isn't diversification — and BRICS partner status carries tariff risk without decision-making power
cards.s: Of RM82.1 billion in local currency settlements, RM62.6 billion (76.2%) is with China. Thailand and Indonesia combined account for RM19.5 billion over 16 years. The BNM-PBOC swap at CNY180 billion / RM110 billion dwarfs all other arrangements. Malaysia became a BRICS partner country in October 2024 — not a full member, with limited practical participation. Trump has threatened 100% tariffs on BRICS-aligned countries reducing USD dominance. Malaysia's semiconductor sector — where 68% of E&E exports to the US are semiconductors, with roughly 65% of those from US-headquartered firms — is directly exposed to this friction. The de-dollarization rhetoric serves domestic political signaling: demonstrating sovereignty, signaling solidarity with the Global South, and maintaining China's goodwill as Malaysia's largest trading partner (USD212 billion annually). The geopolitical risk is asymmetric — the US relationship is where the export revenue concentration sits; the China relationship is where the local currency volume sits.
cards.d: Sources: Parliamentary reply (country breakdown), BNM (swaps), RSIS (BRICS analysis), Lowy Institute, trade minister Tengku Zafrul (semiconductor breakdown). CORRECTED: 68% semiconductor figure now accurately attributed per Stage 3.

cards.h: The 1998 capital controls that saved the ringgit now block its internationalization — and the political incentives to keep them run deeper than economics
cards.s: Mahathir's 1998 controls banned offshore ringgit trading and restricted capital flows. Core restrictions remain: limited offshore trading, restricted hedging channels, and no deep MYR currency-derivatives ecosystem comparable to internationalized currencies. These restrictions hobble the infrastructure needed for ringgit settlement at scale. UCSI economist Liew Chee Yoong warned Anwar's plan 'will falter unless ringgit trade is simplified.' But the controls persist not just from institutional caution — they protect patronage structures that benefit from managed currency markets and controlled capital flows. Full liberalization would expose these networks to market discipline. BNM's RM31.5 billion forex losses in the early 1990s remain institutional memory of what happens when currency ambition outpaces governance. BNM ruled out new capital controls in April 2024 but has not published a roadmap for relaxing the existing ones. Meanwhile, retail payment linkages — DuitNow with Thailand's PromptPay and Indonesia's QRIS — are progressing faster than wholesale settlement, creating a bottom-up de-dollarization track that bypasses the structural constraints.
cards.d: Sources: FMT (Nov 2023 — Liew), BNM (Apr 2024), The Edge (RCI findings), BIS (Jan 2018 — LCSF architecture), BOT (Feb 2025 — LCTF expansion). CORRECTED: removed incorrect Bursa derivatives claim per Stage 3; added patronage dimension per Stage 5; added retail payment linkages per Stage 4.

cards.h: The question isn't whether to reduce dollar dependency — it's whether Malaysia is building yuan dependency instead, and who bears the cost of the transition
cards.text: De-dollarization sounds like sovereignty. In practice, 76% concentration in one partner's currency is not diversification — it's substitution. The commodity pricing trap means Malaysia settles in ringgit but prices in dollars. The hedging gap means companies can't manage the new exposures. And the class dimension is invisible in every headline: B40 Malaysians don't settle trade in any currency — they buy rice, fuel, and medicine priced in a ringgit that lost 26% against the dollar between 2021 and 2024. Sarawak's LNG exports and Sabah's palm oil move through East Malaysian ports but the settlement currency discussion happens entirely in Kuala Lumpur. India's ringgit-rupee framework is a February 2026 handshake with no disclosed volumes. What would change the equation: annual volume disclosure instead of cumulative headlines; a sequenced plan to relax offshore ringgit restrictions; and honest acknowledgment that reduced transaction costs for exporters (real, valuable) is not the same as reduced dollar dependency for the economy (aspirational, structural, decades away).

Malaysia's local currency settlement programme has grown 22.8x since 2009. Within the China corridor, a quarter of bilateral trade now bypasses the dollar. SMEs save on transaction costs. The LCTF expansion to portfolio investment and new appointed dealers is genuine institutional progress. But the programme is being presented with a headline — RM82.1 billion — designed to sound larger than it is. The 76% China concentration creates yuan exposure without corresponding hedging infrastructure. The commodity pricing trap means settlement currency changes but price benchmarks don't. And the 1998-era restrictions that protect the ringgit from speculation also prevent the market depth needed to make it an international settlement currency. The political incentives to maintain this contradiction run across all coalitions. What would make this credible: publish annual volumes by country, not cumulative totals. Separate ASEAN settlement data from China data. Lay out a sequenced plan to build the derivatives and hedging infrastructure that exporters actually need. And stop treating de-dollarization as a sovereignty brand when it is, at best, an incremental plumbing upgrade that saves 2% on transaction fees — real money for SMEs, but not the revolution the headline promises.

sources: Parliamentary reply (Mar 24, 2026 — Malay Mail, FMT, The Star), BNM (swap arrangements, LCSF/LCTF), FMT (Feb 2026 — RMB data; Nov 2023 — Liew critique), BIS (Feb 2026 — BNM Governor; Jan 2018 — Muhammad Ibrahim), AMRO (Dec 2023), RSIS (BRICS analysis), Lowy Institute, Carnegie, The Diplomat (Apr 2025), MATRADE (2024 trade data), The Edge (RCI forex losses), Tengku Zafrul (semiconductor breakdown), BOT (Feb 2025 — LCTF expansion), ECB (global trade invoicing)
confidence: High on RM82.1B, country breakdown, swap sizes, BRICS status (official sources). High on 1998 controls and BNM forex losses (RCI). Medium on commodity pricing trap (industry inference). Medium on semiconductor exposure (ministerial statement, correctly attributed). Low on India ringgit-rupee volumes (framework only).
